Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have an organized study routine most of the time. I'd kind of drift through the week and then realize I had stuff due soon. When I did study, I preferred watching videos or looking at diagrams since that's how things click for me. I'd maybe put in a few hours here and there, but it wasn't structured at all. Sometimes I'd have these random bursts where I'd focus intensely, but that wasn't consistent.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Deadlines were basically my main motivator—if something was due, suddenly I'd find the time. Other weeks I might barely touch anything course-related. With six online courses going on, it was hard to keep any real schedule. I'd just deal with whatever felt most urgent at the moment.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or getting a notification about something new posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I was missing or if grades were posted. I wasn't one of those people who logged in daily out of habit. It was more reactive than proactive, if I'm being honest with myself.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to that point where logging in felt automatic or natural. There were maybe a couple weeks where I tried to be more consistent, but it didn't stick. Life stuff and other courses always seemed to pull my attention elsewhere. The LMS was more of a tool I used when I absolutely had to rather than a daily habit.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched a lot of video minutes because that's genuinely how I absorb information best. Being able to pause, rewind, and see concepts demonstrated visually made things actually make sense. The discussion boards were okay sometimes for seeing how other people understood things, but videos were my go-to.
Q6: I pretty much ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the discussion prompts. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what actually helped me learn versus what felt like busywork. Some of the supplementary materials seemed redundant if I'd already watched the video on the same topic. I'd skim things rather than deeply engage with everything posted.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to exams or assignments, it felt worth my time. Activities that were visual and explained concepts clearly were valuable to me. But if something felt like it was just there to pad out the course or check a box, I'd probably skip it. I needed to see the practical payoff, especially with my limited time and resources.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me most of the time. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later and then never did. There's this mental calculation where if it doesn't count for anything, why would I prioritize it over the million other things demanding my attention? It's not ideal, but that's how it went.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download videos and PDFs to study offline because my internet isn't always reliable. I'd take screenshots of important diagrams and review them on my phone when I was commuting or waiting around. A lot of my actual studying happened with downloaded materials or notes I'd written out by hand. The LMS wouldn't capture any of that.
Q10: There were tons of times like this. I'd download a lecture, watch it multiple times offline while taking notes, and really engage with the material—but the LMS would just show one view. Or I'd be working through problems in a notebook using concepts from the course, but there's no way to track that. My exam scores reflect that learning happened even if my online activity didn't.
Q11: All the time, yeah. I'd access something once, download it, and then that was my main study resource for days or weeks. My internet situation means I can't always be streaming videos or constantly connected. So I'd grab what I needed in one session and then work with it offline. It was more practical for my situation.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. I had some stress going on, and juggling six courses with limited resources was overwhelming. Sometimes I'd just feel this wave of 'what's the point' and disengage for a bit. The mindfulness stuff they introduced actually helped me reset during those times, which surprised me.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd just do the bare minimum on the LMS—log in, grab what I needed, log out. Competing responsibilities from other courses meant I couldn't give equal attention to everything. Sometimes confusion about what was actually required made me procrastinate because I didn't know where to start. Fatigue was real, especially with medium stress levels throughout the semester.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through materials without actually processing anything. Sometimes I'd log in with good intentions but end up just clicking around without really learning. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually engaging.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is why my completion rate was so low. Even knowing something was important, I'd convince myself I had more time or that I worked better under pressure. Sometimes it was anxiety about not doing it well enough, so I'd just... not start. Other times I was genuinely overwhelmed and avoidance felt easier than facing it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire engagement strategy. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active on the LMS. Without that pressure, I'd drift and not log in much. It's not a great system, but the urgency of deadlines was the main thing that got me to actually complete tasks. My activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar content. But honestly, I didn't always check feedback promptly. Sometimes I'd avoid looking at it if I was worried it would be negative. The feedback that was specific and constructive actually helped me understand what to focus on for exams, which probably contributed to my good exam performance.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having six online courses simultaneously made it hard to give this one consistent attention. When modules were clearly organized with videos upfront, I engaged more easily. But when things felt scattered or the expectations weren't clear, I'd disengage and wait until I absolutely had to figure it out. Structure helped when it was there.
Q19: Visual content and clear video explanations made engagement way easier for me. What made it harder was when there was too much text-heavy content or when the path through the material wasn't obvious. Also, discussion requirements felt forced sometimes—I only posted three times because it didn't feel natural to me. The platform itself was fine, but the content format mattered a lot.
Q20: I think that could be someone exploring the LMS, trying to understand what's expected, maybe feeling overwhelmed by all the options. They might be downloading stuff to work on offline, or they could be struggling to actually complete work even though they're trying to engage. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just clicking around feeling lost.
Q21: That's basically me, I think. My exam score was really high even though my LMS activity and assignment completion weren't great. I studied a lot offline with downloaded materials, and I'm good at understanding concepts visually and applying them on exams. The LMS activity doesn't capture the actual learning that happened outside the platform. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, how long they spend. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline learning. They can't tell if someone's actually understanding or just scrolling. They definitely miss the personal circumstances affecting someone's engagement, like stress or resource limitations or competing demands.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious and thinking about concepts even outside of study time. For me, real engagement happened when I was working through problems offline or when a video explanation suddenly made something click. It's more about mental investment than physical presence on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually learned through offline study, or how my resource limitations affected my engagement patterns. It wouldn't capture the stress I was managing or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope. It also can't show that despite appearing disengaged by some metrics, I understood the material well enough to score 98 on the exam. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
